exemplar.title: To what extent does hand dominance affect reaction time of students aged 17+-1 years old measured in milliseconds using human benchmark online reaction time test?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does hand dominance affect reaction time of students aged 17+-1 years old measured in milliseconds using human benchmark online reaction time test?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: Clear evidence of independent thinking in design decisions (e.g., choice of paired t-test)
Consistent personal voice and ownership throughout the investigation
Initiative shown in data collection, processing, and reflective commentary
weaknesses: Justification of topic is fairly generic with limited personal significance
Rationale for choice of age group and participants lacks strong personal context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear independent thinking and personal ownership in selecting the topic, designing the methodology, and interpreting results. While the personal relevance and deeper justification could be more explicitly articulated, the overall sustained engagement and initiative across all stages merit the top band for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Precisely stated and testable research question
Mainly appropriate methodology with sensible controls and paired design
Relevant background information and literature to support rationale
weaknesses: Background lacks critical synthesis and evaluation of sources
Limited randomisation and quantification of key confounders
Ethical considerations omit screenâbased visual strain and gender recruitment balance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question is focused and relevant, and the methodology is generally appropriate. Background literature provides necessary context, though deeper critical analysis is missing. Safety and ethical awareness are present but incomplete. Overall performance aligns with the mid-to-upper band for Exploration, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data set with sufficient measurements
Accurate application of means, standard deviations, and paired t-test
Logical interpretation linking statistical outcome to physiological mechanisms
weaknesses: Consideration of uncertainty is minimal (only manufacturer tolerance stated)
Minor inaccuracies in reporting p-value comparison
Lack of effect size calculation and detailed uncertainty propagation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data collection and processing are appropriate and largely accurate, supporting valid conclusions. However, the treatment of measurement uncertainty is minimal, and reporting slips prevent flawless performance. These strengths and limitations place the work solidly in the 4-mark band for Analysis.

strengths: Conclusion is supported by processed data and statistical findings
Relevant comparison to scientific context (cerebral dominance, Hickâs Law)
Realistic suggestions for improving sample size and procedure
weaknesses: Missing quantitative effect size or confidence intervals
Discussion of limitations lacks depth in methodological detail
Suggestions for improvement are somewhat general rather than highly detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student presents a relevant conclusion with some support from data, integrates findings within the scientific context, and identifies key limitations. Suggestions for improvement are realistic but not highly detailed. Performance across all strands aligns with the solid mid-band for Evaluation.

strengths: Clear overall structure with logical headings and flow
Appropriate use of subject terminology to convey methodology and results
weaknesses: Inconsistent in-text referencing and bibliography formatting
Typographical errors and a truncated figure caption hamper clarity
Occasional formatting issues (font inconsistencies, broken URLs)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is generally clear and well-structured, using appropriate terminology and conventions. However, inconsistent referencing, typographical errors, and formatting issues prevent the highest band. These considerations justify a mid-band score of 3 for Communication.

strandSelections.reasoning: Uncertainty is limited to quoting the websiteâs Â±10 ms device lag and adding Â±5 ms to the means; propagation or graphical representation is absent. This shows only a little consideration beyond stating a manufacturer tolerance.

The student refers to literature on cerebral dominance and Hickâs Law to position findings, demonstrating some relevant comparison. Justification is occasional and not rigorously analysed, so the work fits mid-band.

Sections follow IB IA conventions (Introduction, Method, Results, etc.) and information is present in logical order. Minor duplication and misplaced appendix material prevent a flawless score, so a 3 is appropriate.

Suggestionsâlarger sample, randomising order, broader demographicsâare realistic and linked to stated limitations, though detail (e.g., precise procedural changes) is lacking; hence they are âsomeâ rather than âdetailedâ.

The question is stated precisely (dominant vs non-dominant hand; 17 Â± 1 years; ms; specific tool) and is testable. Minor imprecision remains in the Â±1 year wording and lack of hand-specificity for left-handers, so it is relevant and mostly, but not perfectly, focused.
Key limitations (sample size, uncontrolled fatigue, practice effect) are identified and briefly explained, reflecting a reasonable methodological awareness even if depth is limited.

Controls (device, trials, environment) are sensible and a paired design is suitable, showing a mainly appropriate methodology. Yet sample size, randomisation order and quantification of confounders are weak, so not all significant factors are addressed.

Several peer-review papers and physiological concepts (cerebral dominance, Hickâs Law) are cited; however, discussion is brief and sometimes superficial. Sources are not evaluated and in-text referencing is inconsistent, so the material is mainly appropriate but not fully developed.
Content largely stays on task and avoids tangents, enabling reader focus. Some redundancy (re-stating definitions) slightly obscures clarity, but overall relevance is maintained.

Interpretation links the statistical outcome to neuromuscular explanations and rejects H0 correctly, but depth is moderate and caveats are brief. The reasoning is broadly valid yet not fully detailed, placing it in the middle band.

The report is generally clear: headings, tables and one figure aid comprehension. Typographical errors and occasional formatting issues exist but do not seriously hinder understanding, fitting the 3â4 band (score 3).

The student clearly chose a topic of personal relevance (same year-group peers, simple equipment they had access to) and carried the work through to data collection, processing and reflection. References to decisions such as using a paired t-test and describing practice-order effects show independent thinking beyond following a template. While the investigation itself is not highly original, the consistent personal voice and ownership across all stages merit the top band.

Means, standard deviations and a paired t-test are correctly applied; processed values align with the raw table. Minor reporting slips (mis-stated p-value comparison) mean accuracy is not flawless, but overall the processing is appropriate and largely accurate.

The research is justified mainly in generic terms ("important for sports performance","little is known in teenagers") with scant evidence of why this matters personally to the student. Curiosity is present but modest; therefore only limited personal significance is demonstrated.

Raw tables list all 200 individual measurements for each hand, providing sufficient quantity and relevance to support a conclusion. While a larger sample would aid external validity, the presented data set is complete for the stated aim.

Design choices (paired design, 10 trials, consent form) show some initiative, yet most elements replicate standard classroom practice and rely on an off-the-shelf website. Personal input is evident but not âclear throughoutâ, hence the middle band is not justified.

Ethical consent, anonymity and minimising fatigue are described, reflecting some awareness. Nonetheless, no comment is made on screen-based visual strain or equitable gender recruitment, so the consideration is partial rather than full.

Appropriate SEHS terminology (reaction time, neuromuscular coordination, paired t-test) is used with occasional errors ("fatique"). Citations lack uniform formatting but scientific conventions are broadly followed, warranting a mid-band score.

The conclusion restates the numerical difference and statistical significance, directly supported by processed data. However, quantitative effect size and confidence limits are missing, so support is partial rather than fully detailed.

annotations.comment: Figure 1 caption is truncated midâsentence. Provide a complete description of error bars and axis labels to ensure the graph is selfâexplanatory.
Consider calculating an effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) to quantify the magnitude of the dominant handed advantage, strengthening interpretation of practical significance.
The rationale for using a paired t-test is well articulated, demonstrating appropriate methodological choice for withinâsubject design.
The statement confuses p-values and test statistics. Correct phrasing: âp=0.0028<0.05, therefore reject Hâ,â rather than ât-value is less than significance level.â
The strengths section effectively highlights the benefits of consistent conditions and paired design to reduce variability, reinforcing the studyâs internal validity.
The description of practiceâorder effects is correct, but the suggestion for randomisation lacks detail. Propose a specific counterbalancing method with equal sequences.
Conclusion effectively restates the main finding and links to neuromuscular coordination, offering concise closure to the report.
Conclusion omits effect size or confidence intervals, limiting insight into practical significance. Include these metrics to strengthen support.

The research questionâs age range â17Â±1 yearsâ is ambiguous and could exclude leftâhanded variability. Refine to specify precise ages or separate age groups and explicitly state inclusion of left and right dominant handed participants.
The introduction effectively outlines reaction timeâs importance in sports and neuromuscular coordination, providing clear context for the investigation.
Several sources are cited by author name only (e.g., âChudlerâ) without date or context. Adopt a uniform inâtext referencing system (e.g., APA) and include publication years.
Assertions regarding neural pathway efficiency lack specific citations. When stating physiological mechanisms, link directly to peerâreviewed findings and include precise bibliographic details.
Background paragraphs summarize multiple studies but lack critical synthesis or evaluation of methods. Deepen emphasis on how each source informs the current investigationâs rationale.
The alternative hypothesis cites âScienceDirect, n.d.â without author or date information. Provide full citation and describe why this source is relevant to the predicted effect.
The null hypothesis text would benefit from formal notation (e.g. Hâ: Î¼_dominant â Î¼_nondom = 0) to improve clarity and align with statistical conventions.
Independent and dependent variables are clearly identified with units, facilitating reproducibility of the investigation.
The list of controlled variables is comprehensive but lacks randomisation procedures to address practice-order effects. Incorporate counterbalancing or random order for hand testing.

The websiteâs Â±10 ms device lag is noted, but no uncertainty propagation is applied to means or t-test. Apply error propagation formulas or bootstrap methods to quantify overall measurement uncertainty.
Random sampling of Year 13 students is a strength, reducing selection bias and ensuring homogeneity in age.
Confounding variables list acknowledges gender imbalance but stops short of mitigation strategies. Suggest monitoring participant gender distribution and include as a factor in the analysis.
Materials list omits screen latency calibration. Recommend verifying device input lag independently to ensure accurate reaction time measurements.
The procedure outlines consistent 10 s breaks but doesnât address screen fatigue or visual strain. Include screen brightness and distance controls in ethical considerations.
The safety section addresses fatigue and anonymity but omits discussion of prolonged screen use risks. Expand ethical review to include ocular strain mitigation.
The results table clearly displays means and standard deviations, aiding comparison between dominant and nonâdominant hands.
Participant identifiers in the table mix fonts and symbols, which hampers readability. Use plain numeric labels for clarity.
Include the actual t-statistic value and degrees of freedom (e.g., t(9)=X.XX) in the analysis section to improve statistical transparency.
Future research recommendations note ethical constraints but lack alternative designs. Suggest remote data collection or simulation to explore fatigue effects ethically.
The discussion on how hand dominance interacts with broader neuroscientific theories strengthens relevance, integrating your findings within the scientific context.
Bibliography entries vary in format and lack consistent authorâdate ordering (e.g., n.d. entries). Adopt a standardized citation style (APA or MLA) throughout.
URLs in references are broken across lines, risking incomplete retrieval. Ensure each hyperlink remains intact on one line.

Entirely appropriate and relevant background information

Relevant and fully focused research question
Sufficient relevant raw data supporting detailed conclusion
Basic outline of practical issues only
Some relevant comparison to scientific context

Clear understanding of methodological issues demonstrated

Many errors in terminology and conventions
